straight_beat.name: Winston Delacroix
straight_beat.column:
The Marlins’ five-run surge in the seventh inning felt like a punch to the gut, but the Braves’ six-run response in the bottom of the frame was the kind of resilience that defines this team. You don’t win 11 games in 18 tries without learning how to claw back. The ledger’s a little bloodied, but it’s not bleeding out. That’s how the ledger reads.  

The Braves’ offensive explosion—led by Acuna’s two homers and a trio of doubles—was the kind of firepower that makes you forget the Marlins’ late-game rally. But let’s not kid ourselves: the Marlins’ 5-0 lead in the top of the seventh was a reminder that

optimist.name: Caledonia 'Callie' Peach
optimist.column:
The Braves lost yesterday, but here’s the thing: they didn’t fold. They didn’t flinch. They didn’t let a Marlins team with a .500 record steal a game from them. That’s the kind of grit that builds playoff teams. You see, the scoreboard says Miami 5, Braves 6, but the real story is the way those Braves players kept swinging, kept chasing, kept believing. That’s the heartbeat of this team.  

Now, I know what you’re thinking: “Callie, the Braves are -1 in the division. How’s that optimism?” Well, let’s talk numbers. 11-7 isn’t bad for a team that’s still got 10 games left in the regular season. You can’t discount the fact that they’re in the mix. The NL East is a brutal division, but the Braves have the talent, the depth, and the will to claw their way back. Sure, they lost yesterday, but they’re not done yet.  

Take Ozzie, for instance. That smile he has in the dugout? It’s not just a grin. It’s a promise. A guarantee that this team is built to win. You can’t fake that kind

pessimist.name: Earl Tidwell
pessimist.column:
The Braves had a lead. A real one. Five runs, in the fifth inning. You’d think that’s a recipe for a walk in the park, right? But no. Not with these boys. Not with this team. Not with this front office. They had the game in hand, and they still found a way to lose it. Again.  

You look at the numbers, the 11-7 record, and you think, “Hey, they’re not bad.” But here’s the thing: They’re not good enough. Not close. The NL East is a gauntlet, and these Braves are barely keeping their heads above water. Yesterday’s game against the Marlins was a microcosm of their season. A lead that should’ve been a cushion became a prelude to a collapse. The bullpen, that supposed strength, turned into a liability. Raisel Iglesias? He’s not even the problem. The problem is the entire backend of that relief corps. They
